role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 23.9% of wage bill + 15-1253 Software QA Analysts & Testers 2.2% + 15-1299 Computer Occupations All Other 3.1% (role_mix is 4-digit 5415; for this code — custom programming — the developer share is plausibly higher, est. ~35-40% total)
role_hints.note: AI coding assistants already write large fractions of production code; boilerplate, tests, migrations, documentation and maintenance work automate heavily. Requirements elicitation, architecture judgment, legacy-system spelunking and accountability for production systems persist. Within-role fraction plausibly 0.3-0.5 and rising — anchor it in current AI-coding-tool evidence, not intuition.
Requirements documentation, specs and process mapping automate substantially; stakeholder elicitation and organizational navigation persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3021 Computer & Information Systems Managers 8.3% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.0%
role_hints.note: Status reporting, estimation, sprint mechanics and resource paperwork automate; client escalation and delivery accountability persist. Note AI compresses the manager:developer ratio too.
Client executive relationships and hiring persist; internal ops automate.
Proposal/SOW production automates; relationship selling to enterprise IT buyers persists and is the revenue moat such as it is.
Tier-1 support and internal helpdesk work is among the most automated categories already.

role_hints.role: Admin, HR, finance and unallocated tail
role_hints.oews_basis: long tail beyond the listed top-10 (which sums to ~61% of wages), est. remainder ~25-30%
role_hints.note: Recruiting, bench management, back office — mostly automatable; some tail occupations are additional billable engineers, allocate accordingly.

source_hints.source: Stack Overflow Developer Survey (annual, AI-tools section)
source_hints.why: Largest primary dataset on AI coding-assistant adoption among working developers — direct evidence for current_adoption_pct on the delivery side.
Vendor-side telemetry on AI-written code share — corroborates survey adoption data.

source_hints.source: DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) State of DevOps reports
source_hints.why: Includes measured productivity effects of AI tooling in software delivery organizations.

source_hints.source: Public IT-services firm disclosures (Accenture, EPAM, Globant, Cognizant earnings calls) on AI pricing models
source_hints.why: The listed comparables discuss the hourly-to-outcome pricing shift explicitly — the best documented evidence on who keeps AI savings in custom development.

source_hints.source: Software Equity Group (SEG) annual/quarterly IT services M&A reports
source_hints.why: Named tracker of dev-shop and IT-services deal multiples in the lower middle market.
Covers consulting/dev-services firm valuations; confirm the firm still publishes under this name.

source_hints.source: No dedicated owner-age survey known for dev shops
source_hints.why: Founder demographics skew younger than sector-54 norms — succession pressure is likely weaker than the sector prior; verify with ABS owner-age data rather than assuming the professional-services pattern.

capture_questions: Custom development is billed overwhelmingly as time & materials (hourly/day rates) or staff-augmentation FTE rates — when AI cuts hours per deliverable 30-50%, T&M revenue falls mechanically unless firms flip to fixed-price or outcome pricing. What is the evidenced T&M vs. fixed-price mix in the $1-10M EBITDA tier, and is there any documented successful repricing, or are rate-card deflation and shrinking engagement sizes already visible?

Who owns the client relationship — the firm, or individual senior engineers/account leads whose departure takes the account? Dev shops have modest switching costs (code is documented and transferable by design); assess contract structures (MSAs, multi-year support agreements) as the actual moat.

What stops clients from building custom software in-house with AI-augmented small teams — the historic constraint was engineering scarcity, which AI directly relieves; is the addressable outsourced market itself shrinking?
Offshore competition already prices marginal dev capacity — does AI erase the remaining onshore premium (communication, context) that domestic shops charge, accelerating price convergence downward?
terminal_value_question: Custom programming has no license, no physical act, and a product — working code — that AI increasingly generates end-to-end; the terminal question is whether paid external demand survives as accountability-for-production-systems (integration, maintenance liability, security exposure) once clients can generate software directly. Distinguish sharply: AI cutting a dev shop's delivery cost is the bull case only if fee structures decouple from hours AND clients keep outsourcing; evidence that enterprises are insourcing with AI tools or that T&M rates are deflating means the product itself is being replaced. Which is happening, per current data — not per 2022-era demand?
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate, first order: T&M/hourly and staff-aug FTE billing dominate this code — AI productivity flows to clients as fewer billed hours unless repricing succeeds. Model in pi_dist with the T&M share explicit; the public comparables' own commentary on outcome-based pricing is the best evidence either way.

Sibling boundaries within 5415 (surveys and deal data mix them constantly): 541511 = writing/modifying custom software to client spec (dev shops, staff-aug, web/app development with programming); 541512 = designing/integrating computer systems (MSPs, systems integrators, VARs with services); 541513 = ongoing on-site management of a CLIENT'S facilities/operations; 541519 = residual (software installation, disaster recovery). Self-classification is noisy — many 'IT services' benchmarks and MSP deal trackers span all four; check what a source counts before using it.

n_band is 5,767 — the largest target pool in sector 54 — but the same AI that motivates the roll-up is the direct threat to the revenue base; do not let the large N inflate B without confronting the terminal-value question.
The 2022 receipts vintage predates AI coding assistants entirely; treat historical revenue-per-employee and growth as a pre-AI baseline, not a trend.

EBITDA normalization: bench utilization and contractor pass-through (1099/offshore subcontractors inside revenue) distort margins; staff-aug books are valued materially below productized/managed-services books — segment any multiple evidence by revenue model.
Strategic acquirers (Accenture, Globant, EPAM, Cognizant) and PE both buy dev shops — active_consolidators is genuinely high, but per rule 2 this is B/M evidence only, never support for V or A.
